planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Carolina silverbell is an understory tree that requires acidic soil and performs best with afternoon shade in zone 7–8.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; Carolina silverbell is one of the most beautiful and underused native understory trees of the southern Appalachians, producing pure white, bell-shaped flowers in clusters all along the branches in mid-spring when the tree is still essentially leafless — making the display exceptionally striking. The flowers are an important nectar source for early bumblebees and long-tongued native bees. The four-winged seed pods are ornamental through summer and fall. Fall color is clear yellow. It grows naturally as a subcanopy tree beneath taller hardwoods in moist, acidic ravines and slopes and is genuinely shade-tolerant, making it one of the best small-to-medium flowering trees for the shaded garden or woodland garden. An outstanding substitute for Bradford pear in the southern landscape, providing similar white spring bloom but with genuine wildlife value and a graceful natural form.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; southern magnolia tolerates a range of soils but performs best in fertile, moist, slightly acidic conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; southern magnolia is the defining icon of the Deep South, a massive evergreen tree with leathery, glossy dark green leaves (with rusty-brown undersides) and enormous, intensely fragrant white flowers up to 12 inches across that open from May through September — among the largest flowers of any native North American tree. The tree is native to the coastal plain from North Carolina to Texas and is the state flower and tree of Mississippi and Louisiana. Compact cultivars like 'Little Gem' (20–25 feet, repeat-blooming) and 'Brackens Brown Beauty' make it accessible for smaller gardens. The cone-like fruit with brilliant red seeds is highly ornamental and provides important food for gray squirrels, turkeys, and songbirds. Plant away from foundations, driveways, and structures to allow for its eventual large size.

planting.note: Plant fresh samaras in fall as soon as they ripen (they lose viability quickly) or plant container trees in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; red maple is one of the most adaptable native trees but performs best in moist, slightly acidic soil.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — red maple is one of the most abundant and ecologically important trees in eastern North America, occurring naturally from Canada to Florida in an extraordinary range of conditions including swamps, dry ridges, and ordinary upland sites. It is also one of the first trees to bloom each spring, producing dense clusters of tiny red flowers in February–March before leaves emerge that provide critically important early-season pollen for mining bees emerging from overwintering. The brilliant red fall color — rivaling sugar maple in many individual trees — and the fast growth (2–3 feet per year) make it the most commonly planted shade tree in the eastern US. The samaras ripen in spring (unlike most maples which ripen in fall) and are quickly eaten by squirrels, grosbeaks, and finches. Tolerates flooding, clay, and a wide pH range.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in any moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; devil's walking stick spreads by suckering roots and can colonize large areas over time — site it where spreading is welcome or in contained plantings.

directions: Plant in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; devil's walking stick is one of the most architecturally dramatic native plants in eastern North America — a large, coarse shrub or small tree with massive bipinnate-to-tripinnate compound leaves (sometimes 3–4 feet long) arranged in a palm-like whorl at the top of unbranched, viciously prickle-armed stems. The effect in the landscape is aggressively tropical and primitive — unlike anything else in the native plant palette. In late summer, enormous flat-topped clusters of tiny white flowers (12–18 inches across) provide a spectacular nectar feast for dozens of native bee and butterfly species. The dark purple berries in fall are consumed by more than 100 bird species during fall migration. It suckers to form dense colonies in moist woodland edges — give it space or contain it.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; American snowbell grows naturally along stream banks and in moist woodland borders and requires consistently moist, acidic soil.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; American snowbell is a beautiful and underused native shrub of moist woodland edges and stream banks throughout the Southeast, producing masses of nodding, pure white, bell-shaped flowers with bright yellow anthers in spring — a spectacular display in a shaded garden setting. The blooms are mildly fragrant and attract long-tongued native bees. It grows naturally along stream banks and moist forest edges in the coastal plain from Virginia to Florida and west to Arkansas. Much less commonly cultivated than its Japanese relative (Styrax japonicus), but equally ornamental and far more wildlife-friendly. The gray-brown bark and elegant branching pattern provide winter interest. An outstanding choice for a moist, shaded woodland garden or stream bank planting in the Southeast.
